title: Tapestry sunburst design
subtitle: A radiating circle woven in warm yellows and oranges on a frame loom
excerpt: Work a sunburst or radial pattern in graduated yellows and oranges on a warp of natural undyed wool. The design uses hatching between colour bands to blend the edges softly. The finished piece measures about 18 cm square when taken off the loom.
sourceNotes: Sunburst and radial tapestry designs are among the oldest motifs in the tapestry tradition, appearing across Andean, Coptic, and European medieval tapestry work. Synthesised from traditional tapestry design practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: The vertical threads stretched on the loom before weaving begins. In tapestry weaving, the warp is hidden by the weft and sets the width and sett of the finished piece.
The horizontal yarn passed through the shed from side to side, building up the design row by row. In tapestry, the weft completely covers the warp threads.
A blending technique in tapestry weaving in which two alternating colours interleave at the boundary between colour areas, creating a gradual optical mix rather than a hard edge.
The number of warp threads per centimetre or per inch. Tapestry weaving uses a low sett (about 4 to 6 ends per inch) so the weft can pack down completely to cover the warp.

content.text: A sunburst is worked from the outside edges inward, building up the radiating bands from the bottom of the
warp
to the centre circle, then completing the upper half. The colours graduate from a dark outer ring through oranges to a central yellow-white.
Hatching
between each colour band softens the transition.

items.name: Frame loom, at least 25 cm square
Warp yarn, 8-ply cotton or linen in natural or off-white
Tapestry weft yarn in deep orange (outer ring)
Tapestry weft yarn in mid orange
Tapestry weft yarn in pale yellow or cream (centre)

content.text: Setting up the warp
Warp the frame loom at a
sett

of 4 to 5 ends per inch (about 2 per centimetre). Thread the shed stick through alternate warp threads to open the plain-weave sheds. Weave two or three rows of tabby at the base using a scrap yarn to set the warp spacing evenly before beginning the design.
Weaving the design
Step 1: Outer ring
Sketch the sunburst on paper and transfer the key boundary lines to the warp with a fine pencil line. Weave the deep orange
weft

across the full width at the base. As you build toward the circle edge, begin shaping the ray points by weaving partial rows: weave the central threads and stop, turn back, weave further out, stop, turn back. This stepwise building creates the pointed ray tips.
Step 2: Colour transitions with hatching

At each colour boundary, weave 3 to 4 rows of hatching: alternate one row of the outgoing colour with one row of the incoming colour. The two colours share the same picks across the boundary zone and produce a soft optical blend rather than a hard step. Work hatching at every colour boundary in the sunburst.
Step 3: Central disc

Work the pale yellow or cream weft across the central threads only to build the circular disc at the sunburst centre. The disc should span the middle 30 to 40 percent of the warp width. Gradually narrow the weft rows as you build toward the top of the disc, curving the upper edge by stepping in from both sides. Mirror the lower half to complete the circle.
Finishing

Weave two final rows of scrap tabby to secure the edge. Cut the piece from the loom, leaving 5 cm warp tails. Thread each tail back through the weave using a blunt darning needle. The finished piece can be steamed lightly to even the surface and mounted on a rod or stretched over a canvas frame.

items.problem: The ray tips are stepped rather than pointed
items.cause: The partial rows were too wide, creating staircase edges
items.fix: Make partial rows only 1 or 2 warp threads narrower than the previous row. Very fine shaping requires very small steps at each side.

items.problem: The hatching blends look muddy rather than gradual
items.cause: The two hatching colours are too similar in value
items.fix: Choose hatching pairs with a clear value difference. Two colours at the same lightness blend into a third colour optically; a light and a medium of the same hue will blend more clearly.
